title: Bridge Wheel Encoder Redesign
summary: Redesigned a bridge wheel encoder using 3D printed parts, off-the-shelf hardware, and a programmable Hall effect sensor.
description: Redesigned a corrugated bridge wheel encoder to replace an expensive cast housing and linear magnetic sensor assembly with a mostly 3D printed design using off-the-shelf bearings, a shaft, and a programmable rotational Hall effect sensor.
overview:
This project focused on redesigning a bridge wheel encoder used on a corrugated production line to monitor the amount of paper accumulating on the bridge. As the glued corrugated web travels up to the bridge, it forms a festooned loop. Bridge wheels positioned at the minimum and maximum limits help indicate when the line needs to speed up to reduce excess festooning or slow down to avoid a break.

The original assembly used an expensive cast metal housing and a costly linear magnetic sensor paired with a 10-point disk, where each point was detected as a pulse. The redesigned version replaces that approach with a mostly 3D printed housing and wheel assembly supported by an inexpensive off-the-shelf bearing and shaft. This maintains the strength needed for the wheel assembly, including accidental step loads, while greatly simplifying manufacturing.

For sensing, the redesign uses a compact programmable rotational Hall effect sensor configured to match the existing 10-pulse requirement. This allows the new design to preserve compatibility with the current system while reducing cost, simplifying assembly, and bringing nearly all manufacturing in-house. The result is a more affordable and serviceable bridge wheel encoder with a cost savings of over 70%.
